title: Cross-Compilation
primer:
Real-world shader codebases rarely target a single API. A game might ship on Windows (D3D12/HLSL), PlayStation (GNM/PSSL), iOS (Metal/MSL), and web (WebGL/GLSL) from the same source. Two tools make this tractable:

  • SPIRV-Cross — takes SPIR-V bytecode (Vulkan's IR) and decompiles it to GLSL, HLSL, or MSL. SPIR-V is produced by compiling GLSL with glslang, or HLSL/Slang with DXC. SPIRV-Cross is the backbone of cross-platform engines like Godot.
  • Slang multi-target — Slang compiles a single source to GLSL, HLSL, SPIR-V, DXIL, CUDA, and Metal natively, without an intermediate decompilation step. This preserves semantics more reliably than SPIRV-Cross roundtripping.

The practical porting workflow:

  1. Identify all HLSL/Slang-specific constructs (cbuffer, Texture2D.Sample, mul(), semantics, wave intrinsics).
  2. Map each to its GLSL equivalent from the table built in lessons 16-18.
  3. Handle layout and binding differences (std140 padding, explicit binding=N).
  4. Test for driver-specific gotchas (dFdy sign, precision qualifiers on mobile).

This final lesson asks you to port a complete Blinn-Phong lighting shader from HLSL to GLSL ES 3.0 — the most complex porting task in the course.

challenges.title: Identify porting changes
hints: Replace every float3 with vec3, float4 with vec4, saturate(x) with clamp(x,0.0,1.0), and lerp with mix. Also drop the binding=0 from the uniform block — WebGL2 sets UBO bindings at the API level.
Derive pos = vec3(uv*2.0-1.0, 0.0) and normal = normalize(vec3(uv*2.0-1.0, 1.0)) from gl_FragCoord — no vertex shader varyings are available in this quad renderer.
challenges.unlocks_theory: Structured porting starts with a checklist, not guesswork. The six change categories (type names, function names, uniform blocks, output mechanism, input mechanism, semantic annotations) cover ~95% of HLSL→GLSL work. The remaining 5% is precision qualifiers, extension availability, and API-level differences (binding models, push constants). WebGL2 notably does not support the layout(binding=N) qualifier on uniform blocks — block binding must be set with gl.uniformBlockBinding() at the JavaScript level. Automating this with SPIRV-Cross handles the mechanical translation; Slang eliminates it entirely by generating both targets from one source.

hlsl.note: Original HLSL Blinn-Phong shader — the source being ported.

slang.note: Slang version — compiles to both HLSL and GLSL from this single source.

challenges.title: UV-based fallback: port without varyings
challenges.description:
When porting a lighting shader to a fragment-only environment (like this course's quad renderer), there are no vertex shader varyings to supply position and normal. Write a self-contained GLSL ES 3.0 fragment shader that simulates the Blinn-Phong look using procedurally derived normal and position from gl_FragCoord and u_resolution.

Derive:
- `uv` from gl_FragCoord.xy / u_resolution
- A fake surface position: `vec3(uv * 2.0 - 1.0, 0.0)`
- A fake normal from the UV: `normalize(vec3(uv * 2.0 - 1.0, 1.0))`
- Hardcode lightPos = (1.5, 2.0, 1.0), lightColor = (1.0, 0.9, 0.7), cameraPos = (0.0, 0.0, 3.0)
- albedo = mix(vec3(0.2, 0.5, 0.9), vec3(0.9, 0.3, 0.1), 0.4)

Then implement the full Blinn-Phong lighting model inline.
hints: L = normalize(lightPos - pos), V = normalize(cameraPos - pos), H = normalize(L + V). These are the three direction vectors for Blinn-Phong.
diffuse = clamp(dot(normal,L), 0,1); specular = pow(clamp(dot(normal,H), 0,1), 32.0). Then col = albedo*lightColor*diff + lightColor*spec.
challenges.unlocks_theory: Porting lighting shaders to a fragment-only sandbox requires deriving geometry procedurally — a useful technique in its own right for previewing shading models without a full 3D scene. In production cross-compilation, SPIRV-Cross preserves the original varying linkage; in Slang multi-target, the compiler handles stage interfaces automatically. The lighting math itself is identical regardless of language or pipeline stage: normalize, dot, pow, and clamp are universal.

hlsl.note: Self-contained HLSL pixel shader using the same procedural geometry derivation.

slang.note: Slang version — identical math, HLSL-style names, cross-compiles to GLSL or DXIL.

challenges.title: Animated full-scene port with time
hints: saturate(x) → clamp(x, 0.0, 1.0) and lerp → mix are the two function renames. Everything else is just float3 → vec3.
The final saturate(col) on a vec3 becomes clamp(col, 0.0, 1.0) — GLSL's clamp works on vectors component-wise.
challenges.unlocks_theory: You have now ported a complete animated lighting shader across languages. The pattern is always: rename types, rename functions, replace cbuffer with uniform block, replace semantics with built-ins or varyings, and remove the return value in favor of writing to the output variable. Slang's compiler performs exactly these same transformations automatically when targeting GLSL — and SPIRV-Cross does the same starting from SPIR-V. Understanding the manual process is what lets you debug cross-compilation failures and know when an automated tool is wrong.

slang.note: Slang version — identical to HLSL here since no Slang-specific features are needed.
